symptoms.symptom_name: New self-discovered painless firm scrotal lump, felt above and behind the testicle
symptoms.onset: noticed ~30 minutes ago in the shower

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A painless firm lump superior and posterior to the testis, felt separate from it, is the classic location and quality of a spermatocele of the epididymal head.
A painless mass does not exclude a tumour; a tumour can be painless, and an untrained self-exam cannot reliably localise the lump off the testis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Painless lump with normal overlying skin colour, no redness or warmth

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of pain, tenderness, erythema and warmth argues strongly against an acute infective or inflammatory cause.
A painless swelling with normal skin fits a benign cystic collection.

symptoms.symptom_name: No dysuria, urethral discharge, fever, chills or trauma

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No urinary or systemic infective features lowers the probability of an infective scrotal cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Discrete lump that does not change in size with standing or straining

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A varicocele is a compressible 'bag of worms' that augments with standing and Valsalva; a discrete lump that is constant argues against it, though this is not discriminating without examination.
A stable discrete cyst is consistent with a spermatocele, but does not itself exclude a solid mass.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe acute health anxiety with hyperventilation, triggered by online symptom-checking

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Panic and hyperventilation on discovering a lump, worsened by online cancer research, is an acute health-anxiety reaction; it does not indicate a physical emergency.
Screening negative for self-harm confines the distress to acute health anxiety and rules out a psychiatric-safety emergency.

symptoms.symptom_name: Smooth, tense, ~2 cm spherical cystic mass at the superior pole of the epididymis with the normal testis palpable completely separate from it

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A spherical cystic mass at the epididymal head, separate from a normally-palpable testis, is characteristic of a spermatocele; sealed clinician exam finding unavailable to telehealth.
Feeling the testis separate from the mass argues against a testicular tumour, but the exclusion is completed by ultrasound; the AI cannot obtain this sign.

symptoms.symptom_name: Brilliant transillumination of the mass with a focused light source

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Brilliant transillumination indicates clear fluid rather than solid tissue; a clinician-elicited and clinician-interpreted sign the AI cannot obtain and must not simulate.
A solid tumour does not transilluminate; however, transillumination does not fully exclude a coexisting tumour, hence ultrasound remains required.

symptoms.symptom_name: Discrete anechoic, fluid-filled simple cyst arising from the epididymal head with a normal underlying testis on scrotal ultrasound

investigation_details.expected_result: A discrete anechoic, fluid-filled simple cyst arising from the epididymal head with a normal underlying testis, confirming a spermatocele and excluding a solid tumour
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: An underlying testicular tumour would not be excluded and the patient's fear could not be objectively resolved.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An anechoic simple cyst arising from the epididymal head with a normal testis confirms a spermatocele; imaging never available in real time via telehealth.
Ultrasound demonstrating a normal underlying testis is the definitive exclusion of an occult solid tumour — the key sealed investigation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Characterises the scrotal mass, confirms a benign epididymal cyst and excludes an underlying testicular tumour — central to the in-person pathway the AI arranges, and something the AI cannot itself perform.

Palpation, feeling the testis separate from the mass, and transillumination separate a benign cyst from a solid tumour; not obtainable over telehealth, and not reliably obtainable by the patient's own untrained self-exam.
Considered only if ultrasound raises suspicion of a testicular tumour; not a first-line telehealth action and requires venous bloods the AI cannot obtain.
A spermatocele is a non-infective benign cyst; antibiotics are not indicated and the AI must not prescribe them remotely.
